solution:
Solution. We first show that
\[
1-\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{7}-\frac{1}{10}+\cdots+\frac{(-1)^{n+1}}{3 n-2}+\cdots=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{3}} .
\]

Note that
\[
\frac{1}{1+t^{3}}=\sum_{n=1}^{k}(-1)^{n+1} t^{3 n-3}+\frac{(-1)^{k} t^{3 k}}{1+t^{3}}
\]
for any \( t \neq-1 \).
Integrate from 0 to 1 to obtain
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{3}}-\sum_{n=1}^{k}(-1)^{n+1} \int_{0}^{1} t^{3 n-3} d t=(-1)^{k} \int_{0}^{1} \cdot \frac{t^{3 k} d t}{1+t^{3}} .
\]

Hence
\[
\left|\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{3}}-\sum_{n=1}^{k} \frac{(-1)^{n+1}}{3 n-2}\right|=\left|\int_{0}^{1} \frac{t^{3 k} d t}{1+t^{3}}\right| \leq \int_{0}^{1} t^{3 k} d t=\frac{1}{3 k+1} .
\]

Letting \( k \rightarrow \infty \), we get
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n+1}}{3 n-2}=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{3}} .
\]

This integral can be evaluated by partial fractions:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{3}} & =\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{1}\left[\frac{1}{1+t}+\frac{2-t}{1-t+t^{2}}\right] d t \\
& =\frac{1}{3}\left[\log (1+t)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1-t+t^{2}\right)+\sqrt{3} \arctan \frac{2 t-1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]_{0}^{1} \\
& =\frac{1}{3}\left[\log 2+\sqrt{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right]=\frac{1}{3}\left(\log 2+\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. By the same method it can be shown that
\[
\frac{1}{a}-\frac{1}{a+b}+\frac{1}{a+2 b}-\frac{1}{a+3 b}+\cdots=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{t^{a-1} d t}{1+t^{b}}
\]
whenever \( a, b>0 \), a result due to Gauss.

descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We first show that
\[
1-\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{7}-\frac{1}{10}+\cdots+\frac{(-1)^{seqindex+1}}{3 seqindex-2}+\cdots=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d dummyvar}{1+dummyvar^{3}} .
\]

Note that
\[
\frac{1}{1+dummyvar^{3}}=\sum_{seqindex=1}^{partialk}(-1)^{seqindex+1} dummyvar^{3 seqindex-3}+\frac{(-1)^{partialk} dummyvar^{3 partialk}}{1+dummyvar^{3}}
\]
for any \( dummyvar \neq-1 \).
Integrate from 0 to 1 to obtain
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d dummyvar}{1+dummyvar^{3}}-\sum_{seqindex=1}^{partialk}(-1)^{seqindex+1} \int_{0}^{1} dummyvar^{3 seqindex-3} d dummyvar=(-1)^{partialk} \int_{0}^{1} \cdot \frac{dummyvar^{3 partialk} d dummyvar}{1+dummyvar^{3}} .
\]

Hence
\[
\left|\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d dummyvar}{1+dummyvar^{3}}-\sum_{seqindex=1}^{partialk} \frac{(-1)^{seqindex+1}}{3 seqindex-2}\right|=\left|\int_{0}^{1} \frac{dummyvar^{3 partialk} d dummyvar}{1+dummyvar^{3}}\right| \leq \int_{0}^{1} dummyvar^{3 partialk} d dummyvar=\frac{1}{3 partialk+1} .
\]

Letting \( partialk \rightarrow \infty \), we get
\[
\sum_{seqindex=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{seqindex+1}}{3 seqindex-2}=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d dummyvar}{1+dummyvar^{3}} .
\]

This integral can be evaluated by partial fractions:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d dummyvar}{1+dummyvar^{3}} & =\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{1}\left[\frac{1}{1+dummyvar}+\frac{2-dummyvar}{1-dummyvar+dummyvar^{2}}\right] d dummyvar \\
& =\frac{1}{3}\left[\log (1+dummyvar)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1-dummyvar+dummyvar^{2}\right)+\sqrt{3} \arctan \frac{2 dummyvar-1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]_{0}^{1} \\
& =\frac{1}{3}\left[\log 2+\sqrt{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right]=\frac{1}{3}\left(\log 2+\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. By the same method it can be shown that
\[
\frac{1}{parama}-\frac{1}{parama+paramb}+\frac{1}{parama+2 paramb}-\frac{1}{parama+3 paramb}+\cdots=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{dummyvar^{parama-1} d dummyvar}{1+dummyvar^{paramb}}
\]
whenever \( parama, paramb>0 \), a result due to Gauss.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We first show that
\[
1-\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{7}-\frac{1}{10}+\cdots+\frac{(-1)^{posterity+1}}{3 posterity-2}+\cdots=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d riverbank}{1+riverbank^{3}} .
\]

Note that
\[
\frac{1}{1+riverbank^{3}}=\sum_{posterity=1}^{chandelier}(-1)^{posterity+1} riverbank^{3 posterity-3}+\frac{(-1)^{chandelier} riverbank^{3 chandelier}}{1+riverbank^{3}}
\]
for any \( riverbank \neq-1 \).
Integrate from 0 to 1 to obtain
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d riverbank}{1+riverbank^{3}}-\sum_{posterity=1}^{chandelier}(-1)^{posterity+1} \int_{0}^{1} riverbank^{3 posterity-3} d riverbank=(-1)^{chandelier} \int_{0}^{1} \cdot \frac{riverbank^{3 chandelier} d riverbank}{1+riverbank^{3}} .
\]

Hence
\[
\left|\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d riverbank}{1+riverbank^{3}}-\sum_{posterity=1}^{chandelier} \frac{(-1)^{posterity+1}}{3 posterity-2}\right|=\left|\int_{0}^{1} \frac{riverbank^{3 chandelier} d riverbank}{1+riverbank^{3}}\right| \leq \int_{0}^{1} riverbank^{3 chandelier} d riverbank=\frac{1}{3 chandelier+1} .
\]

Letting \( chandelier \rightarrow \infty \), we get
\[
\sum_{posterity=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{posterity+1}}{3 posterity-2}=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d riverbank}{1+riverbank^{3}} .
\]

This integral can be evaluated by partial fractions:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d riverbank}{1+riverbank^{3}} & =\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{1}\left[\frac{1}{1+riverbank}+\frac{2-riverbank}{1-riverbank+riverbank^{2}}\right] d riverbank \\
& =\frac{1}{3}\left[\log (1+riverbank)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1-riverbank+riverbank^{2}\right)+\sqrt{3} \arctan \frac{2 riverbank-1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]_{0}^{1} \\
& =\frac{1}{3}\left[\log 2+\sqrt{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right]=\frac{1}{3}\left(\log 2+\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. By the same method it can be shown that
\[
\frac{1}{quagmire}-\frac{1}{quagmire+synthesizer}+\frac{1}{quagmire+2 synthesizer}-\frac{1}{quagmire+3 synthesizer}+\cdots=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{riverbank^{quagmire-1} d riverbank}{1+riverbank^{synthesizer}}
\]
whenever \( quagmire, synthesizer>0 \), a result due to Gauss.

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We first show that
\[
1-\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{7}-\frac{1}{10}+\cdots+\frac{(-1)^{nothingness+1}}{3 nothingness-2}+\cdots=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d timeless}{1+timeless^{3}} .
\]

Note that
\[
\frac{1}{1+timeless^{3}}=\sum_{nothingness=1}^{infinitum}(-1)^{nothingness+1} timeless^{3 nothingness-3}+\frac{(-1)^{infinitum} timeless^{3 infinitum}}{1+timeless^{3}}
\]
for any \( timeless \neq-1 \).
Integrate from 0 to 1 to obtain
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d timeless}{1+timeless^{3}}-\sum_{nothingness=1}^{infinitum}(-1)^{nothingness+1} \int_{0}^{1} timeless^{3 nothingness-3} d timeless=(-1)^{infinitum} \int_{0}^{1} \cdot \frac{timeless^{3 infinitum} d timeless}{1+timeless^{3}} .
\]

Hence
\[
\left|\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d timeless}{1+timeless^{3}}-\sum_{nothingness=1}^{infinitum} \frac{(-1)^{nothingness+1}}{3 nothingness-2}\right|=\left|\int_{0}^{1} \frac{timeless^{3 infinitum} d timeless}{1+timeless^{3}}\right| \leq \int_{0}^{1} timeless^{3 infinitum} d timeless=\frac{1}{3 infinitum+1} .
\]

Letting \( infinitum \rightarrow \infty \), we get
\[
\sum_{nothingness=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{nothingness+1}}{3 nothingness-2}=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d timeless}{1+timeless^{3}} .
\]

This integral can be evaluated by partial fractions:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d timeless}{1+timeless^{3}} &=\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{1}\left[\frac{1}{1+timeless}+\frac{2-timeless}{1-timeless+timeless^{2}}\right] d timeless \\
& =\frac{1}{3}\left[\log (1+timeless)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1-timeless+timeless^{2}\right)+\sqrt{3} \arctan \frac{2 timeless-1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]_{0}^{1} \\
& =\frac{1}{3}\left[\log 2+\sqrt{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right]=\frac{1}{3}\left(\log 2+\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. By the same method it can be shown that
\[
\frac{1}{fluctuating}-\frac{1}{fluctuating+steadfastless}+\frac{1}{fluctuating+2 steadfastless}-\frac{1}{fluctuating+3 steadfastless}+\cdots=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{timeless^{fluctuating-1} d timeless}{1+timeless^{steadfastless}}
\]
whenever \( fluctuating, steadfastless>0 \), a result due to Gauss.

garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We first show that
\[
1-\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{7}-\frac{1}{10}+\cdots+\frac{(-1)^{hqlvtswo+1}}{3 hqlvtswo-2}+\cdots=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d zpdjfmxq}{1+zpdjfmxq^{3}} .
\]

Note that
\[
\frac{1}{1+zpdjfmxq^{3}}=\sum_{hqlvtswo=1}^{ufbzrcne}(-1)^{hqlvtswo+1} zpdjfmxq^{3 hqlvtswo-3}+\frac{(-1)^{ufbzrcne} zpdjfmxq^{3 ufbzrcne}}{1+zpdjfmxq^{3}}
\]
for any \( zpdjfmxq \neq-1 \).
Integrate from 0 to 1 to obtain
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d zpdjfmxq}{1+zpdjfmxq^{3}}-\sum_{hqlvtswo=1}^{ufbzrcne}(-1)^{hqlvtswo+1} \int_{0}^{1} zpdjfmxq^{3 hqlvtswo-3} d zpdjfmxq=(-1)^{ufbzrcne} \int_{0}^{1} \cdot \frac{zpdjfmxq^{3 ufbzrcne} d zpdjfmxq}{1+zpdjfmxq^{3}} .
\]

Hence
\[
\left|\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d zpdjfmxq}{1+zpdjfmxq^{3}}-\sum_{hqlvtswo=1}^{ufbzrcne} \frac{(-1)^{hqlvtswo+1}}{3 hqlvtswo-2}\right|=\left|\int_{0}^{1} \frac{zpdjfmxq^{3 ufbzrcne} d zpdjfmxq}{1+zpdjfmxq^{3}}\right| \leq \int_{0}^{1} zpdjfmxq^{3 ufbzrcne} d zpdjfmxq=\frac{1}{3 ufbzrcne+1} .
\]

Letting \( ufbzrcne \rightarrow \infty \), we get
\[
\sum_{hqlvtswo=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{hqlvtswo+1}}{3 hqlvtswo-2}=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d zpdjfmxq}{1+zpdjfmxq^{3}} .
\]

This integral can be evaluated by partial fractions:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d zpdjfmxq}{1+zpdjfmxq^{3}} & =\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{1}\left[\frac{1}{1+zpdjfmxq}+\frac{2-zpdjfmxq}{1-zpdjfmxq+zpdjfmxq^{2}}\right] d zpdjfmxq \\
& =\frac{1}{3}\left[\log (1+zpdjfmxq)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1-zpdjfmxq+zpdjfmxq^{2}\right)+\sqrt{3} \arctan \frac{2 zpdjfmxq-1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]_{0}^{1} \\
& =\frac{1}{3}\left[\log 2+\sqrt{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right]=\frac{1}{3}\left(\log 2+\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. By the same method it can be shown that
\[
\frac{1}{xjovdylm}-\frac{1}{xjovdylm+prwkseha}+\frac{1}{xjovdylm+2 prwkseha}-\frac{1}{xjovdylm+3 prwkseha}+\cdots=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{zpdjfmxq^{xjovdylm-1} d zpdjfmxq}{1+zpdjfmxq^{prwkseha}}
\]
whenever \( xjovdylm, prwkseha>0 \), a result due to Gauss.

kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1 - Absolute convergence and factorisation  
Since  
\[
(2p+1)2^{2p+1},\;\;(3q+1)3^{3q+1},\;\;(5r+1)5^{5r+1}\xrightarrow[p,q,r\to\infty]{}\infty
\]
exponentially, the multiple series converges absolutely.  By Tonelli's
theorem it can be rearranged as the product  

\[
S=A\;B\;C,
\qquad\text{where}\qquad
\begin{aligned}
A&=\sum_{p=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^p}{(2p+1)2^{2p+1}},\\[2mm]
B&=\sum_{q=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^q}{(3q+1)3^{3q+1}},\\[2mm]
C&=\sum_{r=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^r}{(5r+1)5^{5r+1}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

  
Step 2 - The quadratic block \(A\)  

The Maclaurin expansion \(\arctan x=\sum_{n\ge0}(-1)^n x^{2n+1}/(2n+1)\)
gives immediately  

\[
\boxed{\;A=\arctan\dfrac12\;} .
\]

  
Step 3 - The cubic block \(B\)  

3.1  Integral representation  
\[
B=\frac13\int_{0}^{1}\frac{\mathrm dt}{1+t^{3}/27}
  =\int_{0}^{1/3}\frac{\mathrm du}{1+u^{3}},
  \qquad u=\frac t3 .
\]

3.2  Decomposition of \(1/(1+u^{3})\)  
Because \(1+u^{3}=(1+u)(u^{2}-u+1)\),

\[
\frac1{1+u^{3}}
     =\frac13\Bigl(\frac1{1+u}+\frac{2-u}{1-u+u^{2}}\Bigr),
\]
and therefore  

\[
\boxed{
B= \frac23\ln2-\frac16\ln7
   +\frac{\pi}{6\sqrt3}
   -\frac{\sqrt3}{3}\arctan\frac1{3\sqrt3}}
   \;\approx\;0.3300942253 .
\]

The numerical value agrees to \(10^{-10}\) with a direct summation of
the defining series for \(B\).

  
Step 4 - The quintic block \(C\)

4.1  Integral representation  
\[
C=\frac15\int_{0}^{1}\frac{\mathrm dz}{1+z^{5}/5^{5}}
  =\int_{0}^{1/5}\frac{\mathrm du}{1+u^{5}},
  \qquad z=5u .
\]

4.2  Cyclotomic factorisation  
Introduce the golden ratio \(\varphi=(1+\sqrt5)/2\) and set  

\[
\alpha=\frac1\varphi=2\cos\frac{2\pi}{5},\qquad
\beta =-\varphi      =2\cos\frac{4\pi}{5},
\]
so that  

\[
1+u^{5}=(u+1)\bigl(u^{2}+\alpha u+1\bigr)\bigl(u^{2}+\beta u+1\bigr).
\]

4.3  Real partial fractions  
Solving  

\[
\frac1{1+u^{5}}
     =\frac{A}{u+1}
      +\frac{Bu+C}{u^{2}+\alpha u+1}
      +\frac{Du+E}{u^{2}+\beta u+1},
\]
one obtains the unique real coefficients  

\[
A=\frac15,\quad
B=\frac{5-\sqrt5}{10\sqrt5},\quad
C=E=\frac25,\quad
D=-\frac{5+\sqrt5}{10\sqrt5}.
\]

4.4  Antiderivative needed  
For \(|\gamma|<2\),

\[
\int \frac{p(2u+\gamma)+q}{u^{2}+\gamma u+1}\,\mathrm du
      =p\ln(u^{2}+\gamma u+1)
       +\frac{2q}{\sqrt{4-\gamma^{2}}}\;
        \arctan\frac{2u+\gamma}{\sqrt{4-\gamma^{2}}}.
\]

Write \(Q_\alpha(u)=u^{2}+\alpha u+1,\;
      Q_\beta(u)=u^{2}+\beta u+1\) and  

\[
\Delta_\alpha=\sqrt{4-\alpha^{2}}
             =\sqrt{\frac{5+\sqrt5}{2}},\qquad
\Delta_\beta =\sqrt{4-\beta ^{2}}
             =\sqrt{\frac{5-\sqrt5}{2}} .
\]

Splitting the numerators \(Bu+C\) and \(Du+E\) into a derivative part
and a constant part gives  

\[
\begin{aligned}
Bu+C&=\frac{B}{2}\bigl(2u+\alpha\bigr)
      +q_\alpha,\qquad
q_\alpha=C-\frac{\alpha B}{2}
       =\frac14\Bigl(1+\frac1{\sqrt5}\Bigr),\\[4pt]
Du+E&=\frac{D}{2}\bigl(2u+\beta\bigr)
      +q_\beta ,\qquad
q_\beta =E-\frac{\beta D}{2}
       =\frac14\Bigl(1-\frac1{\sqrt5}\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

4.5  Evaluation between \(u=0\) and \(u=1/5\)  
Putting the pieces together,

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle
\begin{aligned}
C&=\frac15\ln\frac65
     +\frac{5-\sqrt5}{20\sqrt5}\,
      \ln\!\Bigl(1+\frac{\alpha}{5}+\frac1{25}\Bigr)
     -\frac{5+\sqrt5}{20\sqrt5}\,
      \ln\!\Bigl(1+\frac{\beta }{5}+\frac1{25}\Bigr)\\[6pt]
  &\quad
     +\frac{1+\tfrac1{\sqrt5}}{2\Delta_\alpha}
      \Bigl[
         \arctan\!\frac{2/5+\alpha}{\Delta_\alpha}
        -\arctan\!\frac{\alpha}{\Delta_\alpha}
      \Bigr]
     +\frac{1-\tfrac1{\sqrt5}}{2\Delta_\beta}
      \Bigl[
         \arctan\!\frac{2/5+\beta}{\Delta_\beta}
        -\arctan\!\frac{\beta}{\Delta_\beta}
      \Bigr]\\[8pt]
  &\approx 0.199\,989\,334\,8\;.
\end{aligned}}
\]

(The additional factor \(1/2\) in front of the two arctan brackets is
crucial; without it the value of \(C\) is incorrect.)

A direct numerical summation of the defining series for \(C\) confirms
the above value to at least eleven decimal places.

  
Step 5 - Final product  

\[
\boxed{
S=\arctan\!\frac12\;
   \Bigl(
        \tfrac23\ln2-\tfrac16\ln7
       +\tfrac{\pi}{6\sqrt3}
       -\tfrac{\sqrt3}{3}\arctan\tfrac1{3\sqrt3}
    \Bigr)
   \times
   \Bigl[
        \text{expression for }C\text{ in Step 4.5}
   \Bigr]} .
\]

For reference, the individual numerical values are  

\[
A\approx0.463\,647\,6090,\qquad
B\approx0.330\,094\,2253,\qquad
C\approx0.199\,989\,3348,
\]
and therefore  

\[
\boxed{\;S\approx0.030\,607\,874\;}\quad
(\text{correct to }10^{-9}).
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
(1) Dimensional escalation – the single-series prototype has been replaced by
a triple series; absolute convergence and Fubini’s theorem must be justified
before any factorisation is allowed.

(2) Multiple interacting structures – each of the three factor-sums carries a
different arithmetical modulus (2, 3, 5) and a different geometric weight
(\(2^{2p+1},3^{3q+1},5^{5r+1}\)).  The solver must recognise three distinct
analytic devices: a classical power-series identification (quadratic block),
a non-trivial partial-fraction integral for a cubic denominator, and a fully
cyclotomic decomposition for a quintic denominator.

(3) Deeper theory – the cubic integral already needs a real partial-fraction
expansion that produces both logarithms and arctangents; the quintic integral
demands a factorisation through the fifth roots of \(-1\) and the systematic
use of quadratic real factors, bringing in the golden ratio
(\(\alpha,\beta\)) and requiring care with branch choices of the arctangent.
One is forced to handle irreducible quadratics, to exploit the identity
\(\displaystyle\int\! \dfrac{dx}{x^{2}+bx+1}\), and to keep track of the
various constants that appear at the lower limit.

(4) Length and subtlety – while the original problem is finished after a
single substitution and a short partial-fraction line, here the solver must
perform the whole procedure three times, each time on a denominator of higher
degree; the last step (degree 5) is already at the edge where the real
partial-fraction method begins to be unwieldy, yet it remains elementary and
explicit.

(5) No pattern matching shortcut – none of the three blocks coincides with a
standard tabulated constant; the final answer is a product of three
independent, structurally different elementary expressions.  Direct guessing
or mechanical lookup is virtually impossible; the full chain of reductions is
indispensable.

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1 - Absolute convergence and factorisation  
Since  
\[
(2p+1)2^{2p+1},\;\;(3q+1)3^{3q+1},\;\;(5r+1)5^{5r+1}\xrightarrow[p,q,r\to\infty]{}\infty
\]
exponentially, the multiple series converges absolutely.  By Tonelli's
theorem it can be rearranged as the product  

\[
S=A\;B\;C,
\qquad\text{where}\qquad
\begin{aligned}
A&=\sum_{p=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^p}{(2p+1)2^{2p+1}},\\[2mm]
B&=\sum_{q=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^q}{(3q+1)3^{3q+1}},\\[2mm]
C&=\sum_{r=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^r}{(5r+1)5^{5r+1}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

  
Step 2 - The quadratic block \(A\)  

The Maclaurin expansion \(\arctan x=\sum_{n\ge0}(-1)^n x^{2n+1}/(2n+1)\)
gives immediately  

\[
\boxed{\;A=\arctan\dfrac12\;} .

3.1  Integral representation  
\[
B=\frac13\int_{0}^{1}\frac{\mathrm dt}{1+t^{3}/27}

  \qquad u=\frac t3 .
\]

3.2  Decomposition of \(1/(1+u^{3})\)  
Because \(1+u^{3}=(1+u)(u^{2}-u+1)\),

\[
\frac1{1+u^{3}}
     =\frac13\Bigl(\frac1{1+u}+\frac{2-u}{1-u+u^{2}}\Bigr),
\]
and therefore  

\[
\boxed{
B= \frac23\ln2-\frac16\ln7
   +\frac{\pi}{6\sqrt3}
   -\frac{\sqrt3}{3}\arctan\frac1{3\sqrt3}}
   \;\approx\;0.3300942253 .
\]

The numerical value agrees to \(10^{-10}\) with a direct summation of
the defining series for \(B\).

  
Step 4 - The quintic block \(C\)

4.1  Integral representation  
\[
C=\frac15\int_{0}^{1}\frac{\mathrm dz}{1+z^{5}/5^{5}}
  =\int_{0}^{1/5}\frac{\mathrm du}{1+u^{5}},
  \qquad z=5u .
\]

4.2  Cyclotomic factorisation  
Introduce the golden ratio \(\varphi=(1+\sqrt5)/2\) and set  

\[
\alpha=\frac1\varphi=2\cos\frac{2\pi}{5},\qquad
\beta =-\varphi      =2\cos\frac{4\pi}{5},
\]
so that  

\[
1+u^{5}=(u+1)\bigl(u^{2}+\alpha u+1\bigr)\bigl(u^{2}+\beta u+1\bigr).
\]

4.3  Real partial fractions  
Solving  

\[
\frac1{1+u^{5}}
     =\frac{A}{u+1}
      +\frac{Bu+C}{u^{2}+\alpha u+1}
      +\frac{Du+E}{u^{2}+\beta u+1},
\]
one obtains the unique real coefficients  

\[
A=\frac15,\quad
B=\frac{5-\sqrt5}{10\sqrt5},\quad
C=E=\frac25,\quad
D=-\frac{5+\sqrt5}{10\sqrt5}.
\]

4.4  Antiderivative needed  
For \(|\gamma|<2\),

\[
\int \frac{p(2u+\gamma)+q}{u^{2}+\gamma u+1}\,\mathrm du
      =p\ln(u^{2}+\gamma u+1)
       +\frac{2q}{\sqrt{4-\gamma^{2}}}\;
        \arctan\frac{2u+\gamma}{\sqrt{4-\gamma^{2}}}.
\]

Write \(Q_\alpha(u)=u^{2}+\alpha u+1,\;
      Q_\beta(u)=u^{2}+\beta u+1\) and  

\[
\Delta_\alpha=\sqrt{4-\alpha^{2}}
             =\sqrt{\frac{5+\sqrt5}{2}},\qquad
\Delta_\beta =\sqrt{4-\beta ^{2}}
             =\sqrt{\frac{5-\sqrt5}{2}} .
\]

Splitting the numerators \(Bu+C\) and \(Du+E\) into a derivative part
and a constant part gives  

\[
\begin{aligned}
Bu+C&=\frac{B}{2}\bigl(2u+\alpha\bigr)
      +q_\alpha,\qquad
q_\alpha=C-\frac{\alpha B}{2}
       =\frac14\Bigl(1+\frac1{\sqrt5}\Bigr),\\[4pt]
Du+E&=\frac{D}{2}\bigl(2u+\beta\bigr)
      +q_\beta ,\qquad
q_\beta =E-\frac{\beta D}{2}
       =\frac14\Bigl(1-\frac1{\sqrt5}\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

4.5  Evaluation between \(u=0\) and \(u=1/5\)  
Putting the pieces together,

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle
\begin{aligned}
C&=\frac15\ln\frac65
     +\frac{5-\sqrt5}{20\sqrt5}\,
      \ln\!\Bigl(1+\frac{\alpha}{5}+\frac1{25}\Bigr)
     -\frac{5+\sqrt5}{20\sqrt5}\,
      \ln\!\Bigl(1+\frac{\beta }{5}+\frac1{25}\Bigr)\\[6pt]
  &\quad
     +\frac{1+\tfrac1{\sqrt5}}{2\Delta_\alpha}
      \Bigl[
         \arctan\!\frac{2/5+\alpha}{\Delta_\alpha}
        -\arctan\!\frac{\alpha}{\Delta_\alpha}
      \Bigr]
     +\frac{1-\tfrac1{\sqrt5}}{2\Delta_\beta}
      \Bigl[
         \arctan\!\frac{2/5+\beta}{\Delta_\beta}
        -\arctan\!\frac{\beta}{\Delta_\beta}
      \Bigr]\\[8pt]
  &\approx 0.199\,989\,334\,8\;.
\end{aligned}}
\]

(The additional factor \(1/2\) in front of the two arctan brackets is
crucial; without it the value of \(C\) is incorrect.)

A direct numerical summation of the defining series for \(C\) confirms
the above value to at least eleven decimal places.

  
Step 5 - Final product  

\[
\boxed{
S=\arctan\!\frac12\;
   \Bigl(
        \tfrac23\ln2-\tfrac16\ln7
       +\tfrac{\pi}{6\sqrt3}
       -\tfrac{\sqrt3}{3}\arctan\tfrac1{3\sqrt3}
    \Bigr)
   \times
   \Bigl[
        \text{expression for }C\text{ in Step 4.5}
   \Bigr]} .
\]

For reference, the individual numerical values are  

\[
A\approx0.463\,647\,6090,\qquad
B\approx0.330\,094\,2253,\qquad
C\approx0.199\,989\,3348,
\]
and therefore  

\[
\boxed{\;S\approx0.030\,607\,874\;}\quad
(\text{correct to }10^{-9}).
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
(1) Dimensional escalation – the single-series prototype has been replaced by
a triple series; absolute convergence and Fubini’s theorem must be justified
before any factorisation is allowed.

(2) Multiple interacting structures – each of the three factor-sums carries a
different arithmetical modulus (2, 3, 5) and a different geometric weight
(\(2^{2p+1},3^{3q+1},5^{5r+1}\)).  The solver must recognise three distinct
analytic devices: a classical power-series identification (quadratic block),
a non-trivial partial-fraction integral for a cubic denominator, and a fully
cyclotomic decomposition for a quintic denominator.

(3) Deeper theory – the cubic integral already needs a real partial-fraction
expansion that produces both logarithms and arctangents; the quintic integral
demands a factorisation through the fifth roots of \(-1\) and the systematic
use of quadratic real factors, bringing in the golden ratio
(\(\alpha,\beta\)) and requiring care with branch choices of the arctangent.
One is forced to handle irreducible quadratics, to exploit the identity
\(\displaystyle\int\! \dfrac{dx}{x^{2}+bx+1}\), and to keep track of the
various constants that appear at the lower limit.

(4) Length and subtlety – while the original problem is finished after a
single substitution and a short partial-fraction line, here the solver must
perform the whole procedure three times, each time on a denominator of higher
degree; the last step (degree 5) is already at the edge where the real
partial-fraction method begins to be unwieldy, yet it remains elementary and
explicit.

(5) No pattern matching shortcut – none of the three blocks coincides with a
standard tabulated constant; the final answer is a product of three
independent, structurally different elementary expressions.  Direct guessing
or mechanical lookup is virtually impossible; the full chain of reductions is
indispensable.
